title: Ensure That the Log Metric Filter and Alerts Exist for SQL  Instance Configuration Changes

input_row.title: Ensure That the Log Metric Filter and Alerts Exist for SQL  Instance Configuration Changes
input_row.description: It is recommended that a metric filter and alarm be established for SQL instance configuration changes.
input_row.rationale: Monitoring changes to SQL instance configuration changes may reduce the time needed to detect and correct misconfigurations done on the SQL server. Below are a few of the configurable options which may the impact security posture of an SQL instance: • Enable auto backups and high availability: Misconfiguration may adversely impact business continuity, disaster recovery, and high availability • Authorize networks: Misconfiguration may increase exposure to untrusted networks
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console Ensure the prescribed log metric is present: 1. For each project that contains Cloud SQL instances, go to Logging/Logs-based Metrics by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/logs/metrics. 2. In the User-defined Metrics section, ensure that at least one metric is present with the filter text: protoPayload.methodName="cloudsql.instances.update" Ensure that the prescribed alerting policy is present: 3. Go to Alerting by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/monitoring/alerting. 4. Under the Policies section, ensure that at least one alert policy exists for the log metric above. Clicking on the policy should show that it is configured with a condition. For example, Violates when: Any logging.googleapis.com/user/ stream is above a threshold of zero(0) for greater than zero(0) seconds means that the alert will trigger for any new owner change. Verify that the chosen alerting thresholds make sense for the user's organization. 5. Ensure that the appropriate notifications channels have been set up. From Google Cloud CLI Ensure that the prescribed log metric is present: 1. List the log metrics: gcloud logging metrics list --format json 2. Ensure that the output contains at least one metric with the filter set to protoPayload.methodName="cloudsql.instances.update" 3. Note the value of the property metricDescriptor.type for the identified metric, in the format logging.googleapis.com/user/. Ensure that the prescribed alerting policy is present: 4. List the alerting policies: gcloud alpha monitoring policies list --format json 5. Ensure that the output contains at least one alert policy where: • conditions.conditionThreshold.filter is set to metric.type=\"logging.googleapis.com/user/\" • AND enabled is set to true
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console Create the prescribed Log Metric: 1. Go to Logging/Logs-based Metrics by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/logs/metrics and click "CREATE METRIC". 2. Click the down arrow symbol on the Filter Bar at the rightmost corner and select Convert to Advanced Filter. 3. Clear any text and add: protoPayload.methodName="cloudsql.instances.update" 4. Click Submit Filter. Display logs appear based on the filter text entered by the user. 5. In the Metric Editor menu on right, fill out the name field. Set Units to 1 (default) and Type to Counter. This ensures that the log metric counts the number of log entries matching the user's advanced logs query. 6. Click Create Metric. Create the prescribed alert policy: 1. Identify the newly created metric under the section User-defined Metrics at https://console.cloud.google.com/logs/metrics. 2. Click the 3-dot icon in the rightmost column for the new metric and select Create alert from Metric. A new page appears. 3. Fill out the alert policy configuration and click Save. Choose the alerting threshold and configuration that makes sense for the user's organization. For example, a threshold of zero(0) for the most recent value will ensure that a notification is triggered for every owner change in the user's project: Set `Aggregator` to `Count` Set `Configuration`: - Condition: above - Threshold: 0 - For: most recent value 4. Configure the desired notification channels in the section Notifications. 5. Name the policy and click Save. From Google Cloud CLI Create the prescribed log metric: • Use the command: gcloud logging metrics create Create the prescribed alert policy: • Use the command: gcloud alpha monitoring policies create • Reference for command usage: https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/alpha/monitoring/policies/create
input_row.impact: Enabling of logging may result in your project being charged for the additional logs usage. These charges could be significant depending on the size of the organization.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control can be fully verified via GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). The audit process leverages specific commands to check for the existence of logs-based metrics and alert policies in GCP.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_logging_logs_based_metric_sql_instance_configuration_change_alert_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. **Check Logs-based Metrics:**
   - Use the command: 
     ```bash
     gcloud logging metrics list --format json
     ```
   - Validate that the output contains at least one metric with the filter set to `protoPayload.methodName="cloudsql.instances.update"`. This indicates there is a logs-based metric capturing changes to Cloud SQL instance configurations.

2. **Check Alerting Policies:**
   - Use the command:
     ```bash
     gcloud alpha monitoring policies list --format json
     ```
   - Ensure that the output contains at least one alerting policy where:
     - `conditions.conditionThreshold.filter` is set to `metric.type="logging.googleapis.com/user/"`.
     - `enabled` is set to `true`.

3. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass:** The system finds at least one logs-based metric with the specified filter and at least one enabled alert policy referencing the appropriate metric.
   - **Fail:** No logs-based metric or alert policy exists, or the alert policy is not enabled.

By following this automated approach, organizations can efficiently ensure compliance with the requirement for logging SQL instance configuration changes in GCP.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- The classification as Automated is mostly correct, as the majority of the checks can be performed using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). However, the assessment does not account for verifying the correctness of the metric filter and alert policy configuration, which might require manual validation or additional automated checks to ensure the logic aligns with security policies.
- The use of GCP APIs and Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) is appropriate for checking the existence of logs-based metrics and alert policies.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7

- The command `gcloud logging metrics list --format json` is valid for listing logs-based metrics, and the filter for `protoPayload.methodName="cloudsql.instances.update"` is correctly identified for SQL instance configuration changes.
- The command `gcloud alpha monitoring policies list --format json` is technically correct for listing alerting policies, but it is important to note that the use of the `alpha` command might indicate that this feature is not fully stable or available in all environments.
- The program name `gcp_logging_logs_based_metric_sql_instance_configuration_change_alert_enabled` is descriptive but could be more concise. It lacks specificity regarding the alert condition (e.g., 'configuration_change').

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- Consider using `gcloud monitoring policies describe [POLICY_ID]` to verify specific alert conditions, ensuring that the alert is correctly configured to trigger on the desired metric.
- The program name could be improved for clarity and brevity. A suggestion might be `gcp_sql_instance_config_change_alert_check`.
- Ensure that the alert policy not only exists but is also configured with appropriate thresholds and notification channels.
- Verify the scope of the logs-based metric to ensure it covers all relevant SQL instances, as this might require additional filtering or configuration.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- While the automated approach is generally sound, the reliance on `alpha` commands and the lack of detailed validation steps for alert conditions reduce confidence slightly. There is also a need for more comprehensive checks on the configuration details of the metrics and alerts.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- Modify: Enhance the audit approach to include checks for the correctness of metric filters and alert conditions. Consider transitioning from `alpha` commands to stable versions if available. Improve the program name for clarity.
- Re-classify: Consider a hybrid approach, where automated checks are supplemented with manual validation to ensure the configurations meet security policy requirements.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
Both AI opinions agree that the majority of the checks can be automated using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). The necessary commands to verify the existence of logs-based metrics and alert policies are available and can be executed programmatically. The second opinion suggests additional validation for alert conditions, but this can also be automated by further querying specific policy details using the SDK.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
gcp_logging_sql_instance_configuration_change_alert_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: Cloud Logging and Monitoring
- API/CLI Method: 
  - `gcloud logging metrics list --format json`
  - `gcloud monitoring policies list --format json`
- Properties to Check: 
  - For metrics: Ensure presence of `protoPayload.methodName="cloudsql.instances.update"`
  - For alert policies: Check `conditions.conditionThreshold.filter` includes `metric.type="logging.googleapis.com/user/"` and `enabled` is `true`
- Pass Criteria: 
  - At least one logs-based metric with the specified filter exists.
  - At least one alert policy is enabled and correctly references the metric.
- Fail Criteria: 
  - No logs-based metric or alert policy exists, or the alert policy is not enabled.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
The disagreement was minor, focusing on the completeness of the automated checks. By incorporating additional queries to verify alert conditions, the automation can be comprehensive, addressing the concerns raised by the second opinion.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Google_Cloud_Platform_Foundation_Benchmark_v4_0_0
